dimensions.label: Transformation Purpose
dimensions.definition: Why transformation is introduced.

options.label: Simplicity
options.definition: Reduce structural, descriptive, or procedural complexity while preserving the function required for the task.

options.label: Efficiency
options.definition: Reduce time, memory, energy, data, or implementation cost required to carry out the task.

options.label: Feasibility
options.definition: Make the task feasible when it would otherwise be impractical or inoperable.

options.label: Alignment
options.definition: Make different forms, systems, or domains mutually comparable, connectable, or interoperable.

dimensions.selection_semantics: Multiple purposes may coexist; downstream runtimes may choose one primary purpose plus optional secondary purposes.

dimensions.label: Transformation Object
dimensions.definition: What aspect of the object or task is being transformed.

options.label: Formal Structure
options.definition: Change equations, operators, parameterizations, algebraic forms, bases, or other formal structures.

options.label: Problem / Task Formulation
options.definition: Change objectives, variables, constraints, role assignments, or the way the problem or task is formulated.

options.label: Data / Feature Representation
options.definition: Change the representational form of observed or latent data, features, embeddings, or states.

options.label: Domain / Reference Frame
options.definition: Change the domain, coordinate system, scale, or reference frame in which the object is described, analyzed, or processed.

options.label: Conceptual Frame
options.definition: Change the abstraction, analogy, or explanatory frame through which the object or situation is organized and understood.

dimensions.selection_semantics: A transformation may operate on more than one object simultaneously, but one object is often primary.

dimensions.label: Transformation Approach
dimensions.definition: How transformation is carried out.

options.label: Representation Rewrite
options.definition: Rewrite the same object through different variables, codes, or bases while keeping the underlying domain and reference frame unchanged.

children.label: Reparameterization
children.definition: rewrite through a different variable or parameter scheme

children.label: Recoding
children.definition: rewrite in a different code or representational format

children.label: Canonicalization / Normalization
children.definition: rewrite into a standardized or normalized form

children.label: Basis Change
children.definition: rewrite in a different basis while keeping the same domain and reference frame

options.label: Structural Reorganization
options.definition: Transform an object by splitting, merging, or regrouping its internal organization.

children.label: Factorization
children.definition: express the whole through a set of factors

children.label: Partitioning
children.definition: divide the whole into modules, regions, or blocks

children.label: Scale Separation
children.definition: separate the whole into distinguishable scales or resolutions so they can be handled separately

children.label: Aggregation / Coarse-Graining
children.definition: merge fine-grained variables, states, or interactions into coarser units

options.label: Dimension Change
options.definition: Change the representational dimensionality by suppressing or introducing degrees of freedom.

children.label: Projection / Reduction
children.definition: retain fewer directions or degrees of freedom

children.label: Expansion / Lifting
children.definition: introduce additional directions or degrees of freedom

options.label: Domain / Frame Conversion
options.definition: Re-express the same object in a different analysis domain, coordinate system, scale, or reference frame.

children.label: Coordinate / Frame Change
children.definition: rewrite under another coordinate system or reference frame

children.label: Domain Conversion
children.definition: rewrite in a different analysis or computational domain

children.label: Scale Transformation
children.definition: re-express at a different descriptive or analytical scale

options.label: Operational Recasting
options.definition: Rewrite the operational structure of a problem so that a different solver, procedure, or computational route becomes applicable.

children.label: Objective Recast
children.definition: restate what is to be optimized, balanced, or pursued

children.label: Constraint Recast
children.definition: rewrite constraints into a more workable form for optimization, inference, or computation

children.label: Resolution Recast
children.definition: rewrite the problem at a different computational granularity or continuity level

children.label: Surrogate Recast
children.definition: replace the direct object with a functionally adequate surrogate

options.label: Conceptual Reframing
options.definition: Reinterpret the object or situation through a different abstraction, analogy, or explanatory frame.

children.label: Level Reframing
children.definition: shift the level of abstraction to make the situation more general or more concrete

children.label: Analogical Transfer
children.definition: reinterpret through another conceptual source whose pattern guides understanding or design

children.label: Conceptual Integration
children.definition: combine previously separate explanatory frames into a new conceptual structure

dimensions.selection_semantics: Hybrid transformation routes are common; multiple approach families may be combined within one stage.

dimensions.label: Preservation Commitment
dimensions.definition: What must remain preserved for the transformed route to remain valid.

options.label: Equivalence
options.definition: Preserve full formal or operational interchangeability between the transformed and original forms.

options.label: Recoverability
options.definition: Preserve enough information or structure for the original form to be recovered from the transformed one.

options.label: Structural Preservation
options.definition: Preserve relational, topological, or organizational structure across the transformation.

options.label: Constraint Preservation
options.definition: Preserve required bounds, invariants, admissibility conditions, or other explicit constraints.

options.label: Semantic Preservation
options.definition: Preserve the interpretation, intended meaning, or conceptual content of the original form.

options.label: Task Preservation
options.definition: Preserve what the intended task requires in order to remain effective.

options.label: Controlled Approximation
options.definition: Allow bounded loss or simplification under an explicit adequacy or error tolerance.

dimensions.selection_semantics: Preservation commitments are often cumulative rather than exclusive.

paradigm_compass_seeds.definition: Change representation, formulation, or reference frame to make a task more tractable while preserving or controlling what must remain valid.
paradigm_compass_seeds.label: Transformation-based Methodology
paradigm_compass_seeds.trigger_cue: When an alternative representation or formulation makes the task more tractable.
paradigm_compass_seeds.scope_notes: Cross-domain; includes representation change, reformulation, recasting, reframing, and preservation-aware approximation.

dimensions.label: Optimization Role
dimensions.definition: What optimization primarily does in the larger route.

options.label: Fit / Calibrate
options.definition: Adjust internal settings so the system matches observations, targets, or desired behavior.

options.label: Allocate / Distribute
options.definition: Assign limited resources, tasks, or capacities across competing recipients.

options.label: Route / Schedule
options.definition: Choose an ordered path, schedule, or sequence across space or time.

options.label: Design / Configure
options.definition: Choose the system’s structure, topology, layout, composition, or configuration.

options.label: Control / Steer
options.definition: Choose actions or policies in response to evolving state and feedback.

dimensions.selection_semantics: Usually one primary role is chosen for a stage.

dimensions.label: Problem Architecture
dimensions.definition: How the optimization problem is organized before selecting a solver.

subdimensions.label: Optimization Stance

options.label: Extremum Seeking
options.definition: Drive a criterion toward its best attainable value within the admissible solution space.

options.label: Trade-Off Balancing
options.definition: Balance competing criteria that cannot be improved simultaneously.

options.label: Equilibrium Seeking
options.definition: Seek a state sustained by reciprocal balance or best-response relations across coupled components.

options.label: Robustness Hedging
options.definition: Favor solutions that remain acceptable under adverse variation or uncertainty.

subdimensions.definition: What notion of optimality or admissible success is being sought.

subdimensions.label: Feasibility Structure

options.label: Absolute Bounds
options.definition: Impose hard limits whose violation invalidates the candidate outright.

options.label: Soft Feasibility
options.definition: Allow limited violation at explicit cost, penalty, or slack.

options.label: Conditional / Logical Rules
options.definition: Enforce validity through discrete, logical, or implication-based rules.

options.label: Coupled / Dynamic Relations
options.definition: Require consistency across linked variables, flows, or state transitions.

subdimensions.definition: How validity or admissibility is encoded.

subdimensions.label: Decision Object

options.label: Parameters / Configurations
options.definition: Scalar coefficients, gains, thresholds, weights, or configuration settings.

options.label: Allocations / Assignments
options.definition: Resource shares, task allocations, capacity placements, role assignments, or location choices.

options.label: Structures / Architectures
options.definition: Layouts, topologies, module compositions, connectivity patterns, or architectural designs.

options.label: Sequences / Routes / Schedules
options.definition: Ordered paths, visit sequences, timetables, job orders, or action trajectories.

options.label: Policies / Controls / Mechanisms
options.definition: Control laws, decision rules, incentive schemes, pricing rules, or allocation mechanisms.

subdimensions.selection_semantics: One object family is usually primary, but the associated Variable Type axis jointly characterizes its representation.

associated_axes.label: Variable Type
associated_axes.definition: Continuous/discrete structure of the central decision variable regime.

options.label: Continuous
options.definition: Decision variables vary over a continuous range.

options.label: Discrete
options.definition: Decision variables take values from a discrete or countable set.

options.label: Hybrid
options.definition: Decision variables combine coupled continuous and discrete structure in one formulation.

options.label: Mixed
options.definition: Different parts of the formulation use different variable regimes across the decision object.

associated_axes.selection_semantics: Usually singular for a given formulation, though mixed/hybrid values explicitly represent combined regimes.

subdimensions.definition: What is being chosen or optimized.

subdimensions.label: Evaluation Access

options.label: Sampled / Experimental
options.definition: Evaluate through empirical trials or stochastic sampling.

options.label: Direct / Analytic
options.definition: Evaluate using explicit mathematical or logical relations.

options.label: Simulation-Based
options.definition: Evaluate through running mechanistic or dynamic models.

options.label: Proxy / Surrogate
options.definition: Evaluate through a fitted approximation of the target response.

options.label: Judgment-Based
options.definition: Evaluate through elicited preference or human judgment.

subdimensions.definition: How candidate quality can be evaluated.

subdimensions.label: Temporal Commitment

options.label: One-Shot
options.definition: Solve once for a fixed decision episode.

options.label: Stage-Wise
options.definition: Optimize across explicit stages without continuous replanning.

options.label: Receding Horizon
options.definition: Reoptimize a moving future window as the system advances.

options.label: Online / Adaptive
options.definition: Update decisions as new information arrives.

subdimensions.definition: When decisions are committed and revised.

dimensions.selection_semantics: This is a grouping node that contains the stable architectural subdimensions of optimization.

dimensions.label: Solution Logic

options.label: Condition Resolution
options.definition: Obtain solutions by resolving optimality, equilibrium, or self-consistency conditions directly.

children.label: Closed-form resolution
children.definition: resolve directly via a closed analytic expression

children.label: Optimality-system resolution
children.definition: solve coupled optimality or admissibility conditions

children.label: Dual-correspondence resolution
children.definition: solve through a linked dual representation

children.label: Fixed-point / variational resolution
children.definition: solve a self-consistency or variational relation

options.label: Local Refinement
options.definition: Improve a current candidate by exploiting directional, curvature, or neighborhood information around it.

children.label: Directional stepping
children.definition: move along a locally improving direction

children.label: Curvature-shaped update
children.definition: shape the move using local curvature

children.label: Regularized local update
children.definition: update within a stabilizing local neighborhood

children.label: Coordinate / Neighborhood revision
children.definition: revise one local block or coordinate at a time

options.label: Constructive / Branching Search
options.definition: Construct a solution structurally by extending partial components and pruning unpromising continuations.

children.label: Greedy extension
children.definition: add the locally preferred next component

children.label: Branch expansion
children.definition: generate alternative continuations from a partial state

children.label: Feasibility pruning
children.definition: discard partial states that cannot complete validly

children.label: Priority-guided expansion
children.definition: advance the most promising partial states first

options.label: Population / Swarm Search
options.definition: Search with multiple candidate states through variation, retention, and interaction.

children.label: State perturbation
children.definition: Diversify the candidate set by applying structured perturbations to current states or solutions.

children.label: Information recombination
children.definition: combine useful partial traits across candidates

children.label: Quality-based retention
children.definition: Retain candidates that are comparatively stronger under the active objective or fitness signal.

children.label: Collective signaling
children.definition: steer candidates through shared population signals

options.label: Relaxation / Recovery
options.definition: Solve in a softened or enlarged problem space, then recover a valid solution for the original problem.

children.label: Boundary relaxation
children.definition: replace hard boundaries with a more tractable region

children.label: Geometry smoothing
children.definition: replace rough structure with smoother geometry

children.label: Solution recovery
children.definition: map relaxed states back to the original admissible set

children.label: Restriction tightening
children.definition: add valid restrictions to narrow the relaxation gap

options.label: Decomposition / Coordination
options.definition: Divide a large problem into smaller subproblems and coordinate them into a coherent whole.

children.label: Block partitioning
children.definition: split variables or constraints into separable blocks

children.label: Agreement enforcement
children.definition: force local solutions to match on shared quantities

children.label: Shared-signal coordination
children.definition: Coordinate subproblems through shared multipliers, prices, summaries, or coordination signals.

children.label: Hierarchical coordination
children.definition: link upper-level allocation with lower-level response

options.label: Proxy-Guided Search
options.definition: Build and consult an internal proxy of the landscape to decide where real evaluation is most valuable.

children.label: Proxy-surface guidance
children.definition: rank candidate moves on a fitted proxy landscape

children.label: Uncertainty-directed search
children.definition: target regions where uncertainty is informative

children.label: Utility-guided selection
children.definition: choose moves by balancing value and information

children.label: Learned proposal guidance
children.definition: generate candidates from a learned proposal rule

options.label: Lookahead Planning
options.definition: Evaluate current choices by simulating their temporal consequences over future steps or scenarios.

children.label: Forward rollout
children.definition: simulate future consequences from current choices

children.label: Branch evaluation
children.definition: Compare alternative future continuations by scoring their simulated downstream consequences.

children.label: Backward value propagation
children.definition: push downstream value back to earlier decisions

children.label: Rolling replanning
children.definition: replan as the future window moves forward

dimensions.selection_semantics: Hybrid optimization procedures often combine multiple logic families.
dimensions.definition: How the optimization process advances toward a solution.

paradigm_compass_seeds.definition: Choose or construct high-quality solutions by optimizing explicit objectives under constraints.
paradigm_compass_seeds.label: Optimization-based Methodology
paradigm_compass_seeds.trigger_cue: When an objective can be specified and evaluated.
paradigm_compass_seeds.scope_notes: Cross-domain; includes calibration, allocation, design, control, routing, scheduling, and multi-criteria optimization.

dimensions.label: Purpose

options.label: Estimate
options.definition: Infer latent states, parameters, or distributions from available evidence, emphasizing identification of unknown quantities.

options.label: Predict
options.definition: Predict unobserved or future outcomes from available information, emphasizing forecast calibration.

options.label: Decide
options.definition: Compare actions or policies under uncertainty and risk, emphasizing choice under explicit utility or loss criteria.

options.label: Explain
options.definition: Explain observed patterns through causes, mechanisms, or structural dependencies, emphasizing identifiability and intervention.

options.label: Evaluate
options.definition: Assess adequacy, calibration, or mismatch between model and evidence, emphasizing diagnostics and misfit detection.

dimensions.selection_semantics: A probabilistic route may estimate, predict, explain, evaluate, and decide in combination.
dimensions.definition: What the probabilistic route is trying to achieve.

dimensions.label: Probabilistic Formulation

subdimensions.label: Probability Representation

options.label: Parametric
options.definition: Represent uncertainty with a fixed distribution family specified by a finite number of parameters.

options.label: Process-Based
options.definition: Represent uncertainty with a stochastic process over functions, trajectories, or fields.

options.label: Structured-Compositional
options.definition: Represent uncertainty with mixtures, hierarchies, copulas, or other composed distributions.

options.label: Learned-Implicit
options.definition: Represent uncertainty with learned or implicit distributional forms.

options.label: Nonclassical
options.definition: Represent uncertainty with formalisms beyond standard probability theory.

subdimensions.definition: How uncertainty is formally represented.

subdimensions.label: Dependence Structure

options.label: Independent
options.definition: Treat variables as independent, with residual dependence taken as negligible.

options.label: Graphical
options.definition: Represent dependence through local conditional structure.

options.label: Spatial-Field
options.definition: Represent dependence through spatial proximity or field structure.

options.label: Sequential-Temporal
options.definition: Represent dependence through temporal order or trajectories.

options.label: Causal-Interventional
options.definition: Represent dependence through causal relations, interventions, or counterfactuals.

options.label: Relational-Networked
options.definition: Represent dependence through entities, links, or network relations.

subdimensions.definition: How dependence among uncertain quantities is represented.

dimensions.definition: How uncertainty and dependence are represented.

dimensions.label: Inference Scheme

subdimensions.label: Inference Approach

options.label: Bayesian
options.definition: Update uncertainty by combining prior assumptions with evidence.

children.label: Conjugate Bayesian inference
children.definition: Use conjugate prior–likelihood pairs so posterior updates remain in a tractable closed-form family.

children.label: Hierarchical Bayesian
children.definition: Use hierarchical priors to share statistical strength across related groups or levels.

children.label: Nonparametric Bayesian
children.definition: Let effective model complexity grow with the data rather than fixing it in advance.

children.label: Likelihood-free Bayesian
children.definition: Perform Bayesian inference when the simulator is available but a tractable likelihood is not.

options.label: Frequentist
options.definition: Infer through estimators and tests evaluated by repeated-sample properties.

children.label: Likelihood-based inference
children.definition: Infer by optimizing and diagnosing models through their likelihood or log-likelihood.

children.label: Estimation-theoretic inference
children.definition: Focus on point estimators and uncertainty summaries with repeated-sample guarantees.

children.label: Testing-based inference
children.definition: Reason through explicit hypothesis tests, error rates, and decision thresholds.

children.label: Resampling or distribution-free inference
children.definition: Use bootstrap, permutation, rank-based, or other distribution-light repeated-sample procedures.

options.label: Information-Theoretic
options.definition: Infer through information, compression, or divergence principles.

children.label: Maximum-entropy inference
children.definition: Infer distributions by selecting the highest-entropy solution consistent with known constraints.

children.label: Divergence-based inference
children.definition: Infer by minimizing a divergence or discrepancy between model and evidence.

children.label: Compression-based inference
children.definition: Infer by favoring models or explanations with shorter effective description length.

children.label: Information-bottleneck inference
children.definition: Infer by keeping information that is most relevant to the task while discarding the rest.

options.label: Decision-Theoretic
options.definition: Infer in order to choose under explicit loss or utility trade-offs.

children.label: Expected-utility inference
children.definition: choose by expected utility or risk

children.label: Robust or Minimax inference
children.definition: Choose actions or estimates that guard against worst-case or adversarial loss.

children.label: Sequential decision inference
children.definition: Choose under uncertainty while evidence arrives over time and actions affect future information.

children.label: Constrained decision inference
children.definition: Choose under explicit policy, feasibility, or institutional constraints in addition to utility or loss.

subdimensions.definition: What inferential logic governs learning or decision under uncertainty.

subdimensions.label: Inference Engine

options.label: Analytical
options.definition: Use exact or approximate closed-form updates or summaries.

options.label: Sampling
options.definition: Approximate inference through sampling from a target or proposal distribution.

options.label: Optimization-Based
options.definition: Approximate inference by replacing full probabilistic inference with an optimization problem.

options.label: Message Passing
options.definition: Compute by propagating local messages over structured factors.

options.label: Learned-Amortized
options.definition: Approximate inference by learning a reusable inference mapping across repeated cases.

subdimensions.definition: What computational engine is used to realize inference.

dimensions.definition: How probabilistic inference is conceptually framed and computationally realized.

paradigm_compass_seeds.definition: Represent uncertainty explicitly and update belief from evidence to estimate, predict, decide, explain, or evaluate under uncertainty.
paradigm_compass_seeds.label: Probabilistic-based Methodology
paradigm_compass_seeds.trigger_cue: When uncertainty or risk must be quantified.
paradigm_compass_seeds.scope_notes: Cross-domain; includes Bayesian, frequentist, information-theoretic, and decision-theoretic reasoning with multiple inference engines.

dimensions.label: Inversion Objective

subdimensions.label: Inversion Target

options.label: Input / Forcing
options.definition: Recover external inputs, source terms, or forcing signals that drive the observed system.

options.label: State / Field
options.definition: Recover latent states or spatial fields that are not directly observed.

options.label: Parameter / Property
options.definition: Recover static parameters, properties, or coefficients that govern system behavior.

options.label: Structure
options.definition: Recover the equations, operators, graph structure, or topology that define the system.

subdimensions.definition: What hidden object the inverse route aims to recover.

subdimensions.label: Solution Form

options.label: Point
options.definition: Return one best estimate of the unknown object.

options.label: Set / Interval
options.definition: Return a feasible set or interval of admissible solutions.

options.label: Distribution
options.definition: Return a probability distribution over possible solutions.

subdimensions.definition: What form the inverse answer takes.

dimensions.definition: What unknown is to be recovered and in what solution form.

dimensions.label: Forward Model Class

subdimensions.label: Forward Class

options.label: Linear Operator
options.definition: Use a linear forward operator linking the unknown to the observed data.

options.label: Nonlinear Operator
options.definition: Use an explicit nonlinear forward relation between the unknown and the observed data.

options.label: Simulator
options.definition: Use a runnable simulator when the forward relation is available mainly through execution rather than closed-form evaluation.

subdimensions.definition: Primary class of forward relation used in the inverse route.

subdimensions.label: Emulation Layer

options.label: Emulator / Surrogate
options.definition: Approximate the forward model with a cheaper or more tractable proxy while preserving behavior relevant to inversion.

children.label: Emulator / Surrogate
children.definition: approximate the forward model with a cheaper or more tractable proxy while preserving inversion-relevant behavior

subdimensions.selection_semantics: Activate only when a surrogate or emulator is explicitly introduced into the inverse route.
subdimensions.definition: Optional proxy model layer used when the forward model is expensive or difficult to query directly.

dimensions.definition: Container axis for the forward relation family and any optional emulation layer used in inversion.

dimensions.label: Inversion Stabilization

options.label: Evidence Enrichment
options.definition: Stabilize inversion by expanding, diversifying, or adaptively selecting observations.

children.label: View/condition diversity
children.definition: reobserve under new angles or conditions

children.label: Sampling density
children.definition: Reduce instability by adding denser, repeated, or more informative measurements.

children.label: Complementary modalities
children.definition: Reduce ambiguity by combining evidence from different observation types or sensing channels.

children.label: Adaptive acquisition
children.definition: choose the next measurement to maximize information gain

options.label: Structural Restriction
options.definition: Stabilize inversion by imposing domain-agnostic structural assumptions on admissible solutions.

children.label: Smoothness regularization
children.definition: Favor solutions that vary smoothly instead of changing abruptly from point to point.

children.label: Sparsity regularization
children.definition: favor solutions with few active components

children.label: Piecewise regularization
children.definition: favor region-wise simplicity with sharp boundaries

children.label: Manifold restriction
children.definition: restrict solutions to a prescribed low-dimensional manifold

options.label: Domain Constraints
options.definition: Stabilize inversion by enforcing rules and conditions tied to the domain or task.

children.label: Process rules
children.definition: Enforce mechanism, transition, or process relations that admissible solutions must satisfy.

children.label: Balance rules
children.definition: preserve totals, budgets, or conserved quantities

children.label: Context conditions
children.definition: anchor the solution to known external conditions or setups

children.label: Validity rules
children.definition: Exclude states, structures, or reconstructions that violate domain-validity conditions.

options.label: Learned Constraints
options.definition: Stabilize inversion by restricting or guiding solutions using information learned from data.

children.label: Generative priors
children.definition: constrain solutions to a learned data manifold

children.label: Score-based guidance
children.definition: guide solutions using learned gradients of plausibility

children.label: Learned regularization
children.definition: penalize implausible solutions with a learned prior or penalty

children.label: Retrieval guidance
children.definition: anchor reconstruction to similar examples or cases

dimensions.selection_semantics: Multiple stabilization families may be combined.
dimensions.definition: What stabilization burdens are introduced to make inversion well-posed enough for use.

dimensions.label: Inversion Approach

options.label: Direct Analytic
options.definition: Recover the unknown through an explicit inverse map or transform.

options.label: Optimization-Based
options.definition: Recover the unknown by optimizing an inverse objective under priors or regularization.

options.label: Probabilistic Inference
options.definition: Recover the unknown through uncertainty-aware inference over a posterior or related belief state.

options.label: Learned Inverse
options.definition: Recover the unknown through a learned inverse mapping trained on repeated tasks or examples.

dimensions.selection_semantics: Choose the approach whose best-when conditions and limitations fit the current inverse problem.
dimensions.definition: How inversion is solved, represented as a comparison table rather than a simple taxonomy.

paradigm_compass_seeds.definition: Infer hidden causes, inputs, states, parameters, or structures from observed effects through an explicit, simulated, or emulated forward relation.
paradigm_compass_seeds.label: Inversion-based Methodology
paradigm_compass_seeds.trigger_cue: When effects are observed but causes are hidden.
paradigm_compass_seeds.scope_notes: Cross-domain; includes inverse problems with explicit forward models, simulators, surrogates, and multiple stabilization regimes.

dimensions.label: Rule Function

options.label: Judge
options.definition: Derive classifications, diagnoses, or eligibility outcomes from stated conditions.

options.label: Constrain
options.definition: Define and check what is allowed, forbidden, required, or feasible.

options.label: Control
options.definition: Trigger or govern actions, state transitions, or workflow branches under stated conditions.

options.label: Coordinate
options.definition: Prioritize, gate, reconcile, or route among competing rules, modules, candidates, or paths.

dimensions.definition: What rules primarily do in the larger route.

dimensions.label: Rule Type

options.label: Strict
options.definition: A rule applies in categorical form and is not ordinarily graded, weighted, or overridden.

options.label: Defeasible
options.definition: A rule applies by default, but it may be overridden by stronger, more specific, or exceptional rules.

options.label: Graded
options.definition: A rule conclusion holds by degree rather than in pure yes/no form.

options.label: Uncertain
options.definition: A rule conclusion is qualified by explicit weight, confidence, probability, or reliability.

dimensions.definition: What validity mode a rule carries.

dimensions.label: Rule Representation

options.label: Dependency Encoding
options.definition: Encode knowledge by making support, implication, or dependency structure explicit.

children.label: Clausal casting
children.definition: Cast dependencies into explicit antecedent–consequent style units or clauses.

children.label: Typed-schema anchoring
children.definition: bind rule conditions and conclusions to typed roles, schemas, or slots

children.label: Relational-graph structuring
children.definition: externalize dependencies through relation paths or graph structure

options.label: Constraint Encoding
options.definition: Encode knowledge by explicitly restricting the admissible space.

children.label: Bound imposition
children.definition: cast limits or admissible ranges as explicit bounds

children.label: Consistency formulation
children.definition: encode coherence or integrity as co-satisfaction conditions

children.label: Balance formulation
children.definition: cast budgets, capacities, or conserved totals as balance constraints

children.label: Configuration restriction
children.definition: cast allowable structure, topology, or order as explicit constraints

options.label: Prescriptive Encoding
options.definition: Encode knowledge by mapping conditions to prescribed actions, decisions, norms, or preferences.

children.label: Action prescription
children.definition: Encode condition-triggered actions, operations, or policy responses directly in rule form.

children.label: Decision partitioning
children.definition: partition condition space into explicit outcomes or choices

children.label: Norm codification
children.definition: write obligations, permissions, or prohibitions into explicit rules

children.label: Preference externalization
children.definition: make ranking or scoring criteria explicit in rule form

options.label: Temporal-Transition Encoding
options.definition: Encode knowledge by formalizing change, sequence, and persistence explicitly.

children.label: Event qualification
children.definition: Qualify which events or occurrences count as rule-relevant triggers.

children.label: Transition specification
children.definition: write how one state may, must, or must not become another

children.label: Sequence structuring
children.definition: encode order, timing, deadlines, or windows explicitly

children.label: Persistence specification
children.definition: write continuation, expiry, or carry-over conditions into rule form

dimensions.selection_semantics: Hybrid rule encodings are common in real systems.
dimensions.definition: How knowledge is encoded into rules.

dimensions.label: Execution Pattern

options.label: Forward Propagation
options.definition: Run rules by propagating consequences outward from active facts, events, or state changes.

children.label: Match-and-fire propagation
children.definition: match current conditions, then fire consequences

children.label: Change propagation
children.definition: propagate through local updates or events

children.label: Agenda-controlled firing
children.definition: control which applicable rules fire and when

children.label: Closure propagation
children.definition: continue until no further consequences can be derived

options.label: Backward Query
options.definition: Run rules by starting from a query, target, or hypothesis and tracing backward to required support.

children.label: Goal decomposition
children.definition: break the target into rule-relevant subgoals

children.label: Support retrieval
children.definition: retrieve the rules and facts needed for the current target

children.label: Dependency tracing
children.definition: Trace backward along support chains to find the facts or rules that justify a target.

children.label: Justification assembly
children.definition: construct explicit proof, diagnosis, or explanation paths

options.label: Conflict Resolution
options.definition: Run rules by settling competition among simultaneously applicable rules.

children.label: Priority resolution
children.definition: prefer rules with higher rank or authority

children.label: Exception / override resolution
children.definition: let exceptions block or replace defaults

children.label: Specificity / context resolution
children.definition: prefer rules that are more specific or better matched

children.label: Confidence-based resolution
children.definition: settle competing outcomes by certainty, trust, or weight

options.label: Rule-Guided Search
options.definition: Use rules to constrain, rank, gate, or structure exploration of candidate states, actions, plans, or explanations.

children.label: Feasibility pruning
children.definition: eliminate candidates that violate explicit rules

children.label: Gatekeeping
children.definition: Screen candidates before deeper expansion so invalid or low-priority options do not consume search effort.

children.label: Candidate ordering
children.definition: rank alternatives by rule-based relevance or preference

children.label: Expansion control
children.definition: choose which branch or subproblem to expand next

dimensions.selection_semantics: A rule system may combine multiple execution patterns.
dimensions.definition: How rules are executed or consulted at runtime.

paradigm_compass_seeds.definition: Encode explicit rules, constraints, and heuristics for auditable judgment, control, coordination, and search guidance.
paradigm_compass_seeds.label: Rule-based Methodology
paradigm_compass_seeds.scope_notes: Cross-domain; includes logical, constraint-based, prescriptive, and temporal rule systems.

dimensions.label: Fusion Purpose

options.label: Complete
options.definition: Use fusion to combine complementary partial sources into a more complete view of the target, reducing coverage gaps and blind spots.

options.label: Stabilize
options.definition: Use fusion to reduce noise, variance, and source-specific fragility, improving reliability under perturbation and partial source failure.

options.label: Reconcile
options.definition: Use fusion to reconcile conflicting or inconsistent sources into a coherent result, while preserving uncertainty and provenance where disagreement remains.

options.label: Extend
options.definition: Use fusion to expand what can be inferred, explained, or achieved by combining sources, models, or methods in ways that no single component can support alone.

dimensions.selection_semantics: Fusion can simultaneously complete, stabilize, reconcile, and extend.
dimensions.definition: Why fusion is introduced.

dimensions.label: Fusion Locus

subdimensions.label: Input Layer

options.label: Data
options.definition: Fuse raw data, observations, or measurements before higher-level interpretation.

options.label: Constituent Units
options.definition: Fuse physical, material, or other discrete constituents into an integrated whole.

subdimensions.selection_semantics: Optional locus; activate only if fusion occurs at the raw-source or constituent level.
subdimensions.definition: Fusion at the level of raw inputs or constituents.

subdimensions.label: Representation Layer

options.label: Representation
options.definition: Fuse encoded or derived representations, e.g., features, embeddings, or latent states.

options.label: Model
options.definition: Fuse formalized models that represent a target system, phenomenon, or domain.

subdimensions.selection_semantics: Optional locus; activate only if fusion occurs on representations or models.
subdimensions.definition: Fusion at the level of encoded representations or formal models.

subdimensions.label: Operational Layer

options.label: Method
options.definition: Fuse distinct methods or techniques into one integrated approach.

options.label: Workflow
options.definition: Fuse stages, pipelines, or workflows into a coordinated process.

subdimensions.selection_semantics: Optional locus; activate only if methods or workflows are being fused.
subdimensions.definition: Fusion at the level of methods or workflows.

subdimensions.label: Resolution Layer

options.label: Decision
options.definition: Fuse scores, rankings, or candidate actions into final decision outputs.

options.label: Conceptual Frame
options.definition: Fuse theories, concepts, or perspectives into a broader explanatory frame.

subdimensions.selection_semantics: Optional locus; activate only if outputs or explanatory frames are fused.
subdimensions.definition: Fusion at the level of decisions or conceptual resolution.

dimensions.selection_semantics: One or more locus layers may be active; at least one locus layer must be selected in any valid fusion route.
dimensions.definition: Where fusion operates in the larger route.

selection_constraints.definition: At least one fusion locus layer must be active in any valid fusion route.

dimensions.label: Integration Logic

options.label: Aggregative Fusion
options.definition: Fuse by directly aggregating inputs into a combined result.

children.label: Averaging or pooling
children.definition: reduce many inputs to one summary

children.label: Voting or consensus
children.definition: combine judgments into one collective choice

children.label: Stacking or concatenation
children.definition: retain inputs by placing them side by side

options.label: Weighted Fusion
options.definition: Fuse by assigning differential weights across inputs.

children.label: Reliability weighting
children.definition: weight by confidence, uncertainty, or trust

children.label: Relevance or attention weighting
children.definition: weight by salience, importance, or context

children.label: Adaptive or learned weighting
children.definition: learn or update weights from data or feedback

options.label: Alignment-Based Fusion
options.definition: Fuse by making inputs compatible before integration.

children.label: Matching-based alignment
children.definition: align by pairwise, soft, or transport-based correspondence

children.label: Shared-space projection
children.definition: align by projecting inputs into a common space

children.label: Objective-induced alignment
children.definition: align by optimizing agreement or consistency objectives

options.label: Constraint-Guided Fusion
options.definition: Fuse under explicit rules or constraints that govern entry, integration, or conflict resolution.

children.label: Admissibility filtering
children.definition: Screen what may enter fusion by explicit admissibility or entry conditions.

children.label: Constraint-regularized fusion
children.definition: impose feasibility, consistency, or structure during fusion

children.label: Rule-based arbitration
children.definition: Resolve fusion conflicts by explicit precedence, exceptions, or rule-governed choices.

options.label: Joint-Objective Fusion
options.definition: Fuse by solving a shared estimation or optimization problem across inputs.

children.label: Joint estimation
children.definition: estimate shared states, parameters, or assignments

children.label: Shared-latent modeling
children.definition: infer a latent structure from multiple inputs

children.label: Coupled optimization
children.definition: optimize one coupled objective across inputs

options.label: Compositional Fusion
options.definition: Fuse by forming a whole whose properties depend on relations among the parts.

children.label: Configurational composition
children.definition: parts remain distinct; arrangement is decisive

children.label: Coupled composition
children.definition: parts remain distinct; interaction is decisive

children.label: Transformative composition
children.definition: parts are altered or reconstituted in composition

dimensions.definition: How separate sources, models, methods, or stages are integrated.

paradigm_compass_seeds.definition: Integrate multiple sources, models, methods, or perspectives into a coherent or explicitly plural whole with managed conflict and provenance.
paradigm_compass_seeds.label: Fusion-based Methodology
paradigm_compass_seeds.trigger_cue: When evidence is fragmented or conflicting.
paradigm_compass_seeds.scope_notes: Cross-domain; includes input-, representation-, method-, workflow-, decision-, and conceptual-level fusion.

dimensions.label: Simulation Purpose

options.label: Explore
options.definition: Use simulation to probe mechanisms and regime boundaries across scenarios, emphasizing breadth and contrast over pointwise fitting.

options.label: Predict
options.definition: Use simulation as a forward model to project outcomes under specified inputs and assumptions, emphasizing fidelity and repeatability.

options.label: Calibrate
options.definition: Use data and constraints to fit parameters or state variables, emphasizing model-data agreement, residual error, and uncertainty.

options.label: Decide
options.definition: Use simulation to compare interventions and select designs or policies under constraints, emphasizing robustness and decision utility.

dimensions.selection_semantics: A simulation route may explore, predict, calibrate, and decide in combination.
dimensions.definition: Why simulation is introduced.

dimensions.label: World Modeling Approach

subdimensions.label: Primary Formalisms

options.label: Equation-Based Modeling
options.definition: Represent system change with explicit equations and constraints, whose solutions define dynamics in time and, when relevant, in space.

children.label: Lumped-parameter dynamics
children.definition: few aggregated state variables; no explicit spatial resolution

children.label: Spatial field dynamics
children.definition: Represent space-resolved transport, diffusion, wave, or field dynamics across an explicit spatial domain.

children.label: Stochastic dynamics
children.definition: intrinsic randomness via noise or random-jump processes

options.label: Discrete-Event Modeling
options.definition: Represent system change as event-triggered state transitions on an event schedule, capturing queues, resources, and operational constraints.

children.label: Service and queueing systems
children.definition: Represent waiting, contention, capacity, and service dynamics in event-driven systems.

children.label: Workflow and resource processes
children.definition: Represent routing, buffers, synchronization, and resource flow in operational processes.

children.label: Discrete-mode control logic
children.definition: Represent event-driven switching, supervisory logic, or discrete operating modes.

options.label: Agent-Based Modeling
options.definition: Represent the system as interacting entities with local rules or policies; macro behavior emerges from micro decisions and interactions.

children.label: Heterogeneous populations
children.definition: Model many interacting entities with heterogeneous attributes, roles, or contact structures.

children.label: Strategic decision agents
children.definition: Model interacting agents whose local choices are driven by incentives, games, or strategic reasoning.

children.label: Local-interaction swarms
children.definition: Model emergent collective behavior from simple local interaction rules.

subdimensions.definition: Primary formalism used to represent the modeled world.

subdimensions.label: Emulation Layer

options.label: Surrogate Modeling
options.definition: Replace an expensive simulator with a fast emulator that maps inputs to outputs, optionally producing uncertainty estimates for risk-aware analysis and decision-making.

children.label: Reduced-order models
children.definition: Use a compressed low-dimensional state or basis that preserves the simulator behavior most relevant to the task.

children.label: Statistical response surrogates
children.definition: Approximate simulator responses with statistical or regression-style surrogates, often with uncertainty estimates.

children.label: Learned emulators
children.definition: Approximate simulator input–output behavior with a learned data-driven emulator or operator.

subdimensions.selection_semantics: Activate only when a surrogate or emulator is explicitly inserted for speed.
subdimensions.definition: Optional fast-emulator layer used to accelerate a more expensive simulator.

subdimensions.label: Composition Layer

options.label: Hybrid Coupled Modeling
options.definition: Couple multiple component models across formalisms or scales via explicit interfaces exchanging state or events, capturing mixed discrete-continuous behavior.

children.label: Co-simulation coupling
children.definition: Couple separate simulators that exchange state or events under explicit synchronization.

children.label: Multi-physics composition
children.definition: Compose interacting physical subsystems governed by different but coupled formalisms.

children.label: Multi-scale coupling
children.definition: micro ? macro linking; hierarchical nesting

subdimensions.selection_semantics: Activate only when multiple component models are coupled or composed.
subdimensions.definition: Optional coupled-model layer used when multiple models must be composed for scope.

dimensions.definition: How the model world is represented, accelerated, or composed.

dimensions.label: Execution Protocol

options.label: Scenario Runs
options.definition: Run a small, named set of scenarios to compare outcomes and attribute effects to interventions or assumptions.

options.label: Structured Sweep
options.definition: Run a structured sweep over parameters, scenarios, or boundary conditions to map response patterns and trade-offs.

options.label: Uncertainty Propagation
options.definition: Propagate input uncertainty through simulation to obtain output distributions, intervals, and risk measures.

options.label: Sensitivity Scan
options.definition: Perturb inputs locally or globally to rank influential drivers and detect fragile parameters or assumptions.

options.label: Calibration Loop
options.definition: Iteratively update parameters or state variables to match observations under an explicit error model until residuals stabilize.

options.label: Closed Loop Decisions
options.definition: Embed simulation in optimization, planning, control, or learning loops to improve actions or policies over trajectories.

dimensions.selection_semantics: Execution protocols can be combined in one simulation program.
dimensions.definition: How the model world is interrogated or used operationally.

paradigm_compass_seeds.definition: Construct and execute an explicit model world to explore scenarios, project outcomes, calibrate to evidence, or support decisions when direct experimentation is costly or infeasible.
paradigm_compass_seeds.label: Simulation-based Methodology
paradigm_compass_seeds.trigger_cue: When real experiments are infeasible or costly.
paradigm_compass_seeds.scope_notes: Cross-domain; includes equation-based, discrete-event, agent-based, surrogate, and hybrid coupled simulations.

dimensions.label: Evolutionary Role

options.label: Refine
options.definition: Improve an existing solution toward better fit, efficiency, or performance.

options.label: Adapt
options.definition: Adapt to new tasks, environments, operating conditions, or drift.

options.label: Explore
options.definition: Search for novel variants, broaden possible directions, or support open-ended discovery.

dimensions.definition: What evolutionary/adaptive iteration primarily does.

dimensions.label: Update Architecture

subdimensions.label: Update Target

options.label: Parameters / Traits
options.definition: Parameters, thresholds, traits, or other directly adjustable values.

options.label: Structures
options.definition: Update the system by changing components, architectures, topologies, or compositional structure.

options.label: Strategies / Policies
options.definition: Decision, control, routing, or interaction policies.

options.label: Knowledge / Representations
options.definition: Rules, prototypes, memories, latent features, or internal schemas.

subdimensions.definition: What part of the system is being updated.

subdimensions.label: Update Regime

options.label: Episodic
options.definition: Updates occur in isolated episodes or one-off rounds.

options.label: Cyclical
options.definition: Updates occur in staged rounds, batches, or recurring loops.

options.label: Continual
options.definition: Updates accumulate sequentially without full reset.

options.label: Real-Time
options.definition: Updates occur under streaming or immediate-response conditions.

subdimensions.definition: How often and on what schedule updates occur.

subdimensions.label: Update Mechanism

options.label: In-Place Adjustment
options.definition: Adjust the current system directly while preserving its existing structure.

children.label: Calibration
children.definition: re-anchor the current system to a new reference or baseline

children.label: Tuning
children.definition: adjust existing parameters or controls to improve fit, responsiveness, or performance

children.label: Correction
children.definition: counteract a detected bias, drift, disturbance, or local mismatch

options.label: Structural Reconfiguration
options.definition: Change the structure of the current system or the organization among its parts.

children.label: Augmentation
children.definition: attach a new component, stage, or module

children.label: Pruning
children.definition: remove or simplify existing structural components

children.label: Substitution
children.definition: replace a component while preserving its functional slot

children.label: Rewiring
children.definition: reconfigure dependencies, interfaces, or order among retained parts

options.label: Candidate Replacement
options.definition: Update the current system by generating rival candidates and adopting one according to a separate feedback signal.

children.label: Perturbation-based replacement
children.definition: replace with a candidate produced by local perturbation of the current solution

children.label: Recombination-based replacement
children.definition: replace with a candidate assembled from parts of existing candidates

children.label: Proposal-based replacement
children.definition: replace with a candidate produced by an explicit proposal mechanism

options.label: Transfer
options.definition: Update the current system by drawing on externally available models, knowledge, or artifacts.

children.label: Warm-start
children.definition: initialize from an existing model, policy, solution, or prior state

children.label: Distillation
children.definition: transfer by compressing or imitating a richer source

children.label: Importation
children.definition: bring an externally developed artifact into the current system

children.label: Transfer adaptation
children.definition: adapt transferred content for use in the target system

options.label: Meta-Adaptation
options.definition: Update the system by modifying the updater, the internal basis that supports later adaptation, or the governance of future updates.

children.label: Meta-initialization
children.definition: learn starting states or priors optimized for rapid later adaptation

children.label: Update-operator learning
children.definition: learn how feedback or history should be transformed into updates

children.label: Adaptation governance
children.definition: adapt the scope, scheduling, or control settings that govern future updates

children.label: Adaptation scaffolding
children.definition: provide a stable internal basis that makes later adaptation easier

subdimensions.selection_semantics: Multiple update mechanisms may be combined in one adaptive regime.
subdimensions.definition: How the current state is revised, replaced, or extended.

subdimensions.label: Feedback Signal

options.label: Environmental / Interaction
options.definition: Reward, deployment response, closed-loop interaction, or streaming outcomes.

options.label: Objective
options.definition: Loss, fitness, utility, or other explicit objective-based evaluation.

options.label: Comparative
options.definition: Pairwise ranking, relative comparison, tournament, or adversarial comparison.

options.label: Human / Social / Institutional
options.definition: Expert judgment, user preference, social uptake, market acceptance, or institutional endorsement.

subdimensions.selection_semantics: Multiple feedback signals may be combined.
subdimensions.definition: What signal governs selection or revision.

dimensions.definition: How updates are targeted, scheduled, and enacted over time.

dimensions.label: Carryover Logic

options.label: Selective Retention
options.definition: Preserve selected high-performing or validated variants as the primary basis for subsequent rounds.

options.label: Diversity Preservation
options.definition: Retain multiple viable variants to preserve adaptive breadth and avoid premature collapse.

options.label: Forgetting / Refresh
options.definition: Remove, decay, or reset outdated variants, states, or routines under drift or overload.

options.label: Transmission / Exchange
options.definition: Transmit retained variants, traits, modules, or practices into later rounds or other contexts.

dimensions.definition: How information or structure persists across update rounds.

paradigm_compass_seeds.definition: Iterate with feedback through variation, selection, retention, and adaptation to refine, adapt, or explore over time.
paradigm_compass_seeds.label: Evolutionary-based Methodology
paradigm_compass_seeds.trigger_cue: When objectives or context keep shifting.
paradigm_compass_seeds.scope_notes: Cross-domain; includes continual adaptation, transfer, replacement, structural change, and meta-adaptation.

design_notes: Compass seed is the canonical Layer-2 semantic source for paradigm-internal methodological structure. Stable public dimensions live in Layer 1; value spaces, option families, and leaf bullet families live here.

Top-level paradigm anchor semantics are governed by layer1_ontology/um_paradigms_ontology.v1.json; the mirrored top-level fields here are included for local readability and seed self-containment, while the paradigm-internal design space lives in this Compass seed.
Defaults and records should reference compass option paths when they need to commit below the stable dimension level.

Tags are expected for paradigms, dimensions, and stable option families when the human-facing framework supplies a concise subtitle. Leaf bullet options may omit tags when label plus definition is already sufficient.
Leaf bullet families are required to carry definitions; tags remain optional at that depth when the theoretical framework supplies only a concise definitional phrase.
